edges.description: Misfolded TGFBIp accumulates as insoluble deposits in the corneal stroma.
Progressive accumulation of TGFBIp deposits reduces corneal transparency.
TGFBI mutations produce epithelial-stromal corneal dystrophies through mutation-specific amyloid and non-amyloid deposits.
Mutation-specific TGFBIp deposition produces granular corneal dystrophy patterns.
Mutation-specific TGFBIp amyloid deposition produces lattice corneal dystrophy patterns.
Progressive corneal opacification is the pathograph endpoint corresponding to the corneal opacity phenotype.
Progressive opacity reduces corneal transparency and impairs visual acuity.
Deposit extrusion can injure the epithelium and produce recurrent painful erosions.
intermediate_mechanisms: extrusion of stromal deposits into the corneal epithelium

nodes.description: Bilateral symmetric corneal dystrophy with characteristic deposit patterns visible on slit-lamp examination.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of TGFBI corneal dystrophy and molecular diagnostic testing.
evidence.snippet: To date, 70 TGFBI mutations are reported in the Human Gene Mutation Database (HGMD) to cause a spectrum of different epithelial-stromal corneal dystrophies with corneal amyloid and non-amyloid deposits

nodes.description: Progressive corneal opacification due to accumulation of TGFBIp deposits in the corneal stroma. The pattern and morphology of deposits varies by subtype.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic aspects of the TGFBI-associated corneal dystrophies.
evidence.snippet: Corneal dystrophies are a group of inherited disorders localized to various layers of the cornea that affect corneal transparency and visual acuity.

nodes.description: Discrete breadcrumb-like hyaline deposits in the anterior corneal stroma, characteristic of granular CD type I.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of TGFBI corneal dystrophy and molecular diagnostic testing.
evidence.snippet: R555W for granular corneal dystrophy type 1

nodes.description: Branching lattice lines of amyloid in the corneal stroma, pathognomonic for lattice corneal dystrophy type I.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of TGFBI corneal dystrophy and molecular diagnostic testing.
evidence.snippet: R124C for lattice corneal dystrophy type 1

nodes.description: Progressive accumulation of TGFBIp deposits reduces corneal transparency and visual acuity over decades. There is no effective treatment to prevent, halt, or reverse TGFBIp deposition.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic aspects of the TGFBI-associated corneal dystrophies.
evidence.snippet: There is no effective treatment to prevent, halt, or reverse the deposition of TGFBIp.

nodes.description: Recurrent painful epithelial erosions, particularly common in lattice, Reis-Bucklers, and Thiel-Behnke subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Mini-Review: Clinical Features and Management of Granular Corneal Dystrophy Type 2
evidence.snippet: extrusion of the deposits results in ocular pain due to corneal epithelial erosion.

nodes.description: Progressive reduction in visual acuity as deposits accumulate. Severity and age of onset depend on the specific mutation. Homozygous GCD2 (R124H) patients have severe early-onset visual loss.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic aspects of the TGFBI-associated corneal dystrophies.
evidence.snippet: Corneal dystrophies are a group of inherited disorders localized to various layers of the cornea that affect corneal transparency and visual acuity.

evidence.reference_title: Mini-Review: Clinical Features and Management of Granular Corneal Dystrophy Type 2
evidence.snippet: Also, diffuse corneal haze which appears late, causes decrease in visual acuity.

nodes.description: Missense mutations in TGFBI alter the structure of TGFBIp (keratoepithelin), a secreted extracellular matrix protein expressed by corneal epithelial cells. The mutant protein misfolds, disrupting normal protein folding and stability. The severity and age of onset depend on the specific amino acid alteration.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic aspects of the TGFBI-associated corneal dystrophies.
evidence.snippet: The gene product, transforming growth factor β induced protein (TGFBIp) accumulates as insoluble deposits in various forms. The severity, clinicopathogenic variations, age of the onset, and location of the deposits depend on the type of amino acid alterations in the protein.
The codons R124 and R555 of the TGFBI gene were hotspots in Japanese patients

nodes.description: Structurally altered TGFBIp accumulates as insoluble deposits in the corneal stroma. The specific mutation determines the type of deposit: hyaline (granular), amyloid (lattice), or mixed (Avellino). Deposit morphology is mutation-dependent: R555W produces hyaline deposits, R124C produces amyloid lattice deposits, and R124H produces mixed granular-lattice deposits.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic aspects of the TGFBI-associated corneal dystrophies.
evidence.snippet: The deposition of insoluble protein materials in the form of extracellular deposits or intracellular cysts is pathognomic.

evidence.reference_title: Mini-Review: Clinical Features and Management of Granular Corneal Dystrophy Type 2
evidence.snippet: It is characterized by well demarcated granular shaped opacities in central anterior stroma and as the disease progresses, extrusion of the deposits results in ocular pain due to corneal epithelial erosion.
